name: boost-histogram (Fast N-Dimensional Histogramming)

performance.notes: C++ Boost.Histogram backend; 10-100x faster than numpy.histogram for large data

agent_readiness.notes: Fast N-dimensional histograms. import boost_histogram as bh. h = bh.Histogram(bh.axis.Regular(50, -5, 5), bh.axis.Regular(50, -5, 5)). h.fill(x, y). h.values(). h[sum, :] → 1D projection. h[0:5:bh.rebin(5)] → rebinning. h[bh.loc(1.5):bh.loc(2.5)] → value-based slicing. Axes: Regular, Variable, IntCategory, StrCategory, Boolean. Storage: Double, Int64, Weight (for weighted fills). Accumulate: h1 + h2. Serialization: pickle, cloudpickle. Used by hist package (higher-level wrapper). Thread-safe fills. NumPy interface: bh.numpy.histogram2d.

gotchas: Lower-level than hist package — use hist for HEP-style labeled histograms
Slice syntax (bh.loc, bh.rebin, bh.underflow) is non-standard
StrCategory axis stores strings but isn't meant for large cardinality
Thread safety requires care with parallel fills (use atomic storage)
best_when: HEP data analysis histograms (foundation of hist/coffea)
When rebinning, slicing by value, and projections are needed
Building histogram-based data pipelines with serialization
avoid_when: Simple 1D histograms where numpy is sufficient
When labeled histograms (with named axes) are needed (use hist or xhistogram)
summary: boost-histogram provides fast N-dimensional histograms with C++ backend (10-100x vs numpy). bh.Histogram(bh.axis.Regular(50,-5,5)).fill(x). .values(). Value-based slicing: h[bh.loc(1.5):]. Rebinning: bh.rebin(5). Projections: h[sum,:]. Weight/Int64/Double storage. Thread-safe. Pickle serializable. BSD-3 by Scikit-HEP. Foundation of hist package. Best for fast multidimensional histogramming. Use hist for HEP-labeled histograms on top of this.
